rationale: The dominant structure is a large, complex hip-roofed home with multiple intersecting roof planes. Several cues inform the estimate: (1) Shadow length — ground shadows cast from the eastern and southern eaves are moderate in extent relative to the building footprint (roughly 15–20 ft as measured against the 50-ft scale bar), consistent with a mid-range pitch rather than a shallow ranch or a steep colonial. (2) Roof-to-eave geometry — the diagonal hip lines converge to ridges that are noticeably inset from the perimeter eaves, indicating meaningful vertical rise, but the ridges are still broad relative to the plan area, ruling out steep pitches above 8:12. (3) Plane shading contrast — the lighter (sun-facing) and darker (shadowed) roof panels show moderate tonal differentiation, consistent with a 5:12–7:12 slope. (4) Regional context — Newport News, VA is a mid-Atlantic/coastal-plain climate where 5:12–6:12 is the most common residential pitch. Taken together, a 6:12 pitch best reconciles the moderate shadow projection and visible hip geometry.
